%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.6-2 Matrix Algebra

> **Section Objective:** Master the legal moves of matrix algebra — addition, scalar multiplication, multiplication, identity, and inverse — and learn to check dimensions before touching any arithmetic.

Imagine an exam question asks: is the product of a \ (2 \times 3\) matrix and a \ (3 \times 4\) matrix defined? What size is the result? Does reversing the order still work? These are exactly the questions this section prepares you to answer in seconds.

Matrix algebra rewards students who check dimensions first and punish those who assume multiplication works like scalars. Strong students predict the output size before computing a single entry — that habit alone eliminates many impossible answers. By the end of this section you will know when operations are legal, how to compute products correctly, and when an inverse exists.

## 1. Addition and Scalar Multiplication: The Safe Points

Matrix addition and subtraction are the most forgiving operations — but only when the matrices have the **same order**. You add or subtract entry-by-entry: the entry in row \ (i\), column \ (j\) of \ (A + B\) is simply \ (a_{ij} + b_{ij}\). No row-by-column logic, no dimension juggling.

Scalar multiplication is even simpler: multiply every entry of the matrix by the same number \ (c\). The matrix size does not change.

**Key contrast:** addition requires identical dimensions; scalar multiplication imposes no size restriction at all.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Students lose easy marks by attempting to add matrices of different shapes — for example, adding a \ (2 \times 3\) matrix to a \ (3 \times 2\) matrix — just because the individual numbers look manageable. If the orders do not match exactly, the addition is undefined. Check orders first, always.

$$A + B = (a_{ij} + b_{ij})_{m \times n}, \qquad cA = Ac$$
*Addition is performed entry-by-entry and is only defined when both matrices share the same order \ (m \times n\), while scalar multiplication scales every entry by \ (c\) and leaves the matrix dimensions completely unchanged.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Conformability rule: the inner dimensions of A and B must match. The output size is determined by the outer dimensions. One highlighted row of A and one column of B produce exactly one entry \ (c_{ij}\).*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214725719-6790.png)

## 2. Matrix Multiplication: Where Most Mistakes Happen

Matrix multiplication is **not** entry-by-entry. Each output entry \ (c_{ij}\) is the dot product of **row \ (i\) of \ (A\)** with **column \ (j\) of \ (B\)**. You multiply matching entries across the row and column, then sum.

**Conformability rule:** if \ (A\) is \ (m \times n\) and \ (B\) is \ (n \times p\), then \ (AB\) exists and has size \ (m \times p\). The inner dimensions — \ (n\) and \ (n\) — must match. The outer dimensions — \ (m\) and \ (p\) — become the result size.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Reversing the order is not a cosmetic change. \ (AB\) and \ (BA\) may be different sizes, one may be undefined, or both may exist but give different results. Matrix multiplication is **not commutative**.

### EXAM TIP

Strong students predict the product size before computing any entry. Write down \ (m \times n\) times \ (n \times p\) equals \ (m \times p\) as the first step. If the inner dimensions do not match, stop immediately — the product does not exist. This single habit catches many impossible answers before any arithmetic begins.

$$c_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{ik} b_{kj}$$
*This formula means: take row \ (i\) of \ (A\), take column \ (j\) of \ (B\), multiply each pair of matching entries \ (a_{ik}\) and \ (b_{kj}\), then sum all \ (n\) products to get the single output entry \ (c_{ij}\).*

## 3. Properties, Matrix-Vector Multiplication, and Inverse Conditions

**Distributive laws** work as expected: \ ((A+B)C = AC + BC\) and \ (C(A+B) = CA + CB\). These are safe to apply, but remember that order still matters — you cannot swap left and right factors.

**Identity matrix:** the identity \ (I\) satisfies \ (AI = IA = A\), but the identity must have the **correct size** to be conformable with \ (A\). There is no single universal identity — its order depends on context.

**Matrix-vector multiplication** follows the same row-by-column rule. Treat the vector \ (\mathbf{x}\) as an \ (n \times 1\) matrix. The product \ (A\mathbf{x}\) is then \ (m \times 1\). Note that \ (\mathbf{x}A\) is generally undefined here.

**Inverse:** \ (A^{-1}\) exists only when \ (A\) is **square** and \ (\det(A) \neq 0\). If \ (\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{x}\), premultiply both sides by \ (A^{-1}\) to get \ (\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{y}\).

### EXAM TIP

When you see an inverse question, check two things before any computation: is \ (A\) square? Is \ (\det(A) \neq 0\)? If either fails, the inverse does not exist.

$$(A+B)C = AC + BC, \quad C(A+B) = CA + CB, \quad AI = IA = A, \quad |AB| = |A||B|, \quad A^{-1}A = I, \quad \mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{y}$$
*These formulas summarize the core algebra rules for matrix operations, including distributivity, the identity property, the determinant product rule, and the inverse relationship. The inverse formulas \ (A^{-1}A = I\) and \ (\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{y}\) are valid only when \ (A\) is square and \ (\det(A) \neq 0\).*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Always check inner dimensions before multiplying: \ (m \times n\) times \ (n \times p\) gives \ (m \times p\) only.
- Each entry \ (c_{ij}\) comes from row \ (i\) of \ (A\) dotted with column \ (j\) of \ (B\), not entry-by-entry.
- An inverse exists only when \ (A\) is square and \ (\det(A) \neq 0\); check both before computing.

*In the next section we will use these algebra rules to solve larger matrix-based problems.*
